name: Pre-Flood Civilization
description: The Pre-Flood Civilization theory posits that a technologically and/or spiritually advanced civilization existed on Earth before a catastrophic global flood event, often associated with the biblical Flood of Noah or similar flood myths found in various cultures. Proponents suggest this civilization possessed knowledge and capabilities far exceeding those of known ancient cultures, and that evidence of their existence has been deliberately suppressed or misinterpreted by mainstream academia. The origins of the theory can be traced back to 19th-century esoteric and theosophical writings, particularly those of Helena Blavatsky and Ignatius L. Donnelly, who interpreted ancient myths and archaeological anomalies as remnants of this lost civilization. Evidence cited includes megalithic structures like the pyramids of Giza and Stonehenge, advanced ancient maps, intricate metalwork, and alleged out-of-place artifacts (OOPArts). Mainstream archaeology and geology largely reject the Pre-Flood Civilization theory, arguing that the cited evidence can be explained by known historical and geological processes. They attribute megalithic structures to the ingenuity and engineering capabilities of known ancient civilizations, and explain OOPArts as misinterpretations, hoaxes, or naturally occurring formations. The lack of conclusive archaeological evidence for a global, advanced civilization predating the Holocene period (the last 11,700 years) is a primary point of contention.
subtitle: Lost world before the deluge?

claims.claim: Advanced Technology Existed Before the Flood
claims.evidence: Megalithic structures, advanced maps (Piri Reis map), OOPArts.
claims.criticism: Structures attributed to known ancient civilizations; maps are interpretations; OOPArts are often misidentified or hoaxes.

claims.claim: A Global Civilization Existed Before the Flood
claims.evidence: Similarities in myths and symbols across cultures, suggesting a shared origin.
claims.criticism: Similarities can be explained by cultural diffusion, convergent development, or shared human experiences.

claims.claim: The Flood Erased Most Evidence of This Civilization
claims.evidence: Widespread flood myths, geological evidence of past flooding.
claims.criticism: While floods occurred, they don't necessarily erase all traces of advanced civilizations. Lack of substantial archaeological evidence remains a problem.

keyFigures.name: Helena Blavatsky
keyFigures.role: Theosophist who popularized the idea of ancient, advanced civilizations.

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Author promoting theories of lost civilizations and ancient mysteries.

keyFigures.name: Ignatius L. Donnelly
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Atlantis: The Antediluvian World', a foundational text for the theory.

keyFigures.name: Kenneth Feder
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist known for debunking pseudoarchaeological claims.

timeline.event: Publication of Ignatius Donnelly's 'Atlantis: The Antediluvian World'
Theosophical Society promotes the idea of ancient, advanced civilizations.
Popularization of the theory through books, documentaries, and online forums.

sources.author: Feder, Kenneth L.
sources.work: Frauds, Myths, and Mysteries: Science and Pseudoscience in Archaeology
sources.title: Feder, Kenneth L.
